planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; this hybrid handles cold and heat well.

directions: This adaptable hybrid offers bright yellow-green foliage in a rounded shrub. Plant at container depth and water the first season. Yellow flowers appear in spring, and the leaves turn orange-red in fall. It tolerates a wide range of conditions and shears well.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the darkest near-black purple color.

directions: Plant this very dark dwarf in full sun to keep the deep blackish-purple foliage rich. Set it at soil level and water through the first summer. The color glows crimson in autumn light. Its tight mounded habit rarely needs pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun or light shade for a spine-free green form.

directions: This nearly spine-free selection is easier to prune and handle than typical barberry. Plant at nursery depth and water while it establishes. Green summer leaves turn orange-red in fall, with small yellow flowers in spring. Shear as needed to shape the rounded form.

planting.note: Plant in spring; note it is a wheat-rust host and restricted in many grain regions.

directions: Plant this large European species in full sun to part shade and water while it establishes. Drooping clusters of yellow flowers in spring give way to tart red edible berries used in preserves. Be aware it hosts wheat stem rust and is banned in some grain-growing areas. Prune out old wood in late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the fullest berry set.

directions: Plant this dense deciduous species in full sun where its fruit will color best. Set it at nursery depth and water while establishing. Yellow summer flowers are followed by dense clusters of coral-red to salmon berries and vivid fall foliage. Prune lightly in late winter to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in sun or part shade for a dense evergreen hedge.

directions: This spiny evergreen makes an impenetrable barrier hedge in sun or part shade. Plant at container depth and water until established. Narrow dark green leaves back yellow spring flowers, followed by blue-black berries. Its dense thorny growth deters intruders; prune after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to show off the white-bloomed stems.

directions: Plant this species in full sun where its chalky white young stems stand out, especially in winter. Set it at nursery depth and water while it establishes. Yellow spring flowers give way to red berries with a white bloom, and the leaves redden in fall. Prune in late winter to reveal the whitened branches.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the heaviest berry crop.

directions: This hybrid is grown for its exceptionally heavy display of red autumn berries. Plant in full sun at nursery depth and water while it establishes. Yellow summer flowers precede the dense clusters of scarlet fruit that weigh down the arching branches. Prune out old canes in late winter to keep it productive.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or part shade for a low evergreen mound.

directions: This low, spreading evergreen hybrid suits groundcover or foundation planting in sun to part shade. Plant at container depth and water while establishing. Glossy green leaves are silvery beneath, and yellow flowers appear in spring. Its arching mounded habit needs little pruning beyond tidying.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright light with some shelter from harshest sun.

directions: This miniature gold-leaved mound is ideal for containers, troughs, and tight edges. Plant it level with the soil and water through the first summer. The small yellow leaves stay bright with good light while resisting scorch. Its naturally tidy habit needs essentially no pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to hold the upright form and gold color.

directions: Use this slim gold column as a vertical accent in beds or containers. Plant at nursery depth and water the first season. The bright yellow foliage stays vivid in full sun and takes on orange fall tones. Its narrow upright habit needs only occasional trimming of stray shoots.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the best pink-and-purple variegation.

directions: Plant this variegated form in full sun so the purple leaves keep their pink and cream flecking. Set it at container depth and water the first year. The mottled color is boldest on fresh growth. Prune after flowering to encourage new, well-marked shoots.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the richest wine-purple foliage on a large shrub.

directions: This vigorous large hybrid carries deep purple-red foliage on arching branches. Plant in full sun at nursery depth and water the first season. Yellow spring flowers are followed by red berries, and the leaves glow crimson in autumn. Prune to control its considerable size after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest red foliage and best fall color.

directions: This small mounded selection resembles a compact Crimson Pygmy with even better fall color. Plant it level with the soil in full sun and water through the first summer. The rich red-purple leaves ignite to scarlet in autumn. Its dense dwarf habit rarely needs pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; this is a seedless, non-invasive type.

directions: This compact burgundy barberry is bred to be seedless, avoiding the invasiveness of older forms. Plant at soil level in full sun and water the first season. The deep red-purple leaves hold color through summer and redden further in fall. Its tidy mound needs little care.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or part shade for a low evergreen edging.

directions: This dense dwarf evergreen makes a fine low hedge or edging in sun to part shade. Plant at container depth and water while it establishes. The tiny dark leaves form a compact cushion that rarely flowers or needs pruning. It tolerates clipping into neat low borders.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the reddish semi-evergreen foliage.

directions: This spreading hybrid holds much of its glossy reddish foliage into winter in mild areas. Plant in full sun at container depth and water while establishing. Yellow flowers appear in spring against the dark, lustrous leaves. Its low arching habit suits mass plantings; prune lightly to shape.

planting.note: Set dormant plants in early spring or in fall once summer heat breaks.

directions: Plant in a hole as deep as the root ball and twice as wide, keeping the crown level with the surrounding soil. This tough native tolerates poor ground and drought once its roots take hold, so water regularly only through the first season. Flat white-to-pink flower clusters open in late spring, followed by papery reddish seed pods that persist into winter.

planting.note: Best planted in spring where it can reach large size in moist ground.

directions: Give this vigorous West Coast native room to spread and keep it in reliably moist soil, such as a streambank or rain garden. Prune out the oldest canes at the base after flowering to renew the thicket. Rounded clusters of small white flowers appear in late spring and mature into inflated reddish fruit heads.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a well-drained slope or open woodland edge.

directions: Site this mountain-west native in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun to light shade, where it handles dry summers well. Little pruning is needed beyond removing dead wood. Clusters of white flowers open in late spring, and the peeling bark gives winter interest.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a rocky, well-drained spot in sun or dappled shade.

directions: This compact Rocky Mountain shrub thrives in lean, sharply drained soil and tolerates cold and drought. Give it room in a naturalistic border or foundation planting and prune only to shape. Small pinkish-white flower clusters appear in early summer above the lobed leaves.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in spring in full sun for the darkest foliage color.

directions: Plant this large purple-leaf ninebark where it gets full sun, since shade fades the deep burgundy tone toward green. Cut a third of the oldest stems to the ground each spring to keep it dense and vigorous. Pale pink flower clusters contrast against the dark leaves in late spring and give way to red seed pods.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the brightest gold leaves.

directions: Give this golden-leaved selection full sun, though a bit of afternoon shade in hot regions helps prevent leaf scorch. Renewal-prune the oldest canes in early spring to keep the color fresh and the habit tidy. Clusters of white-to-pink flowers open in late spring against the yellow foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the strongest copper new growth.

directions: Set this upright shrub in full sun, where fresh growth emerges coppery orange before deepening to rich red. Prune a portion of old stems at the base each spring to encourage the brightly colored new shoots. Pinkish-white flowers in early summer stand out against the warm-toned leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for compact, dark, finely cut foliage.

directions: This more compact dark-leaf ninebark has lacy, deeply cut foliage and suits smaller borders. Grow it in full sun and thin the oldest stems in spring to maintain its fine texture. Soft pink flower clusters cover the plant in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to show off the glowing leaf centers.

directions: New leaves emerge with a golden-green glow at the center ringed by deep red, so plant in full sun for the boldest contrast. Prune out old canes each spring to keep new, colorful growth coming. Pinkish-white flowers appear in late spring, followed by reddish fruit clusters.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; its small size suits foundations and low hedges.

directions: This dwarf purple ninebark has small, fine-textured burgundy leaves and stays compact without much pruning. Grow it in full sun to hold the dark color and use it in tight spaces or mass plantings. White-to-pink flowers dot the plant in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; very cold-hardy for northern gardens.

directions: Prized for foliage that shifts through orange, gold, and lime before turning red and purple in fall, this shrub is exceptionally cold-hardy. Plant in full sun and prune old stems in spring for the best new color. Small white flower clusters open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for bright golden spring foliage.

directions: Leaves emerge bright gold in spring, mature to lime-green in summer, and turn yellow in fall on this rounded, dense shrub. Grow in full sun and renewal-prune the oldest wood each year. White flower clusters appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; this is a large old-fashioned golden form.

directions: This vigorous, large golden-leaved ninebark makes a good screen or back-of-border specimen. Give it full sun and space to reach full size, and prune hard in early spring if you want to control its height. Clusters of white flowers open in late spring above the yellow leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for rich red-tinted foliage.

directions: Deep red new growth ages to bronze-green on this medium ninebark with a rounded habit. Plant in full sun and thin the oldest canes each spring to keep the foliage colorful. Pink-tinged white flowers appear in early summer, followed by red seed capsules.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; compact enough for small gardens and containers.

directions: This dwarf bronze-maroon ninebark has small leaves and a dense, refined habit that fits tight spaces. Grow in full sun and prune lightly after bloom to maintain shape. Pink-white flowers cover the plant heavily in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for orange new growth deepening to burgundy.

directions: New leaves emerge bright orange and mature to deep wine-red, giving a two-tone effect on this upright shrub. Plant in full sun and renewal-prune to keep the colorful new shoots coming. Light pink flowers open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for vivid red-purple summer color.

directions: This upright ninebark holds intense reddish-purple foliage through the heat of summer better than many older selections. Grow in full sun and prune old canes in spring to keep it full. Pinkish-white flower clusters appear in late spring against the dark leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; a taller golden selection for screening.

directions: A large, cold-hardy golden ninebark that keeps good yellow color into summer. Plant in full sun and give it room, pruning hard in spring only if you need to contain the size. White flower clusters open in late spring above the bright foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to keep the variegated markings bright.

directions: Leaves are irregularly marked with green, gold, and pink-red on this eye-catching variegated ninebark. Grow in full sun for the strongest coloring and prune out any all-green reverting shoots. White-to-pink flowers appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for compact golden foliage.

directions: A dwarf golden version of Tiny Wine, this compact shrub has small yellow leaves and a dense habit for small gardens. Plant in full sun, with light afternoon shade in hot climates to reduce scorch. Pink-white flowers cover the plant in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for warm cherry-toned new growth.

directions: New foliage emerges in warm cherry-red and orange tones that mellow to bronzy green as the season goes on. Plant in full sun and renewal-prune old canes each spring for fresh color. Pink flower clusters appear in early summer.

planting.note: Set out young plants in autumn so roots establish through the mild wet season.

directions: Plant on a slope or in fast-draining soil with the crown at grade, and avoid burying the trunk. Water lightly the first summer, then withhold irrigation once established since soggy roots invite root rot. Expect clouds of light blue flower spikes in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Autumn planting lets this coastal native root before summer drought.

directions: Dig a hole no deeper than the rootball and firm loose, gritty soil around it. Keep summer water minimal after the first year to prevent crown rot. Glossy dark leaves set off dense clusters of medium-blue bloom in spring.

planting.note: Space plants to knit together into a low mat over a season or two.

directions: Plant this trailing form on banks or at the front of a dry border and mulch lightly with gravel. Pinch stray upright shoots to keep the habit flat and spreading. Small holly-like leaves carry lavender-blue flower clusters in spring.

planting.note: A tough chaparral shrub best planted in fall in unamended native soil.

directions: Set into lean, well-drained ground and skip fertilizer, which this native does not need. Give no summer water once established to keep it long-lived. Small wedge-shaped leaves bear pale to whitish spring flowers.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in fast-draining ground for the deepest flower color.

directions: Site in full sun with sharp drainage and plant slightly high to keep the crown dry. Little pruning is needed beyond light shaping right after bloom. The small deeply grooved leaves are studded with intense deep-blue flowers in spring.

planting.note: Give this large island native room; it can grow into a small tree.

directions: Allow generous space and plant where drainage is excellent, as it can reach tree size. Prune young to a single leader if a small tree form is wanted. Broad soft-felted leaves back large fragrant pale-blue flower clusters in late winter and spring.

planting.note: A low coastal form; plant in fall on a well-drained bank.

directions: Plant on slopes or raised beds where water drains quickly away from the crown. Keep irrigation minimal to preserve the tight low habit. Gray-green foliage carries pale blue-lavender flowers in late winter to early spring.

planting.note: A mountain groundcover needing lean soil and cool root run.

directions: Plant into gritty, gravelly soil where it can root down along the stems. It needs excellent drainage and dislikes lowland heat and summer water. Tiny holly-toothed leaves form a dense flat mat topped with blue spring flowers.

planting.note: A hardy western shrub for cold-winter, well-drained sites.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained soil and avoid rich amendments. It fixes its own nitrogen and needs no feeding. Glossy sticky-scented leaves bear frothy white flower clusters in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: A loose foothill shrub for dry banks and native gardens.

directions: Plant on a dry slope where its arching stems have room to spread. Give no summer water once rooted and prune only to shape after flowering. Fragrant white to pale-blue plumes cover the branches in late spring.

planting.note: A hardy Pacific Northwest native for woodland edges.

directions: Plant at a sunny woodland margin in well-drained soil. Reddish stems show best after leaf drop, so limit pruning to light thinning. Airy white flower clusters appear in late spring on the previous year's wood.

planting.note: A large fast chaparral shrub that can be trained tree-like.

directions: Site in full sun with fast drainage and allow ample room to spread. Its green, nearly spineless stems stay handsome year-round. Loose pale-blue flower sprays open in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sharply drained, sunny spot.

directions: Give this shrub lean soil and full sun, planting the crown a touch high. Narrow sticky leaves have a distinctive warty upper surface. Deep-blue flower clusters appear in spring.

planting.note: A stiff-leaved species for dry, well-drained native beds.

directions: Plant into gritty soil and keep it dry through summer to avoid rot. The rigid, spiny-edged holly-like leaves make a good low barrier. Rich blue-violet flowers open in early spring.

planting.note: A large southern-California chaparral shrub for hot dry banks.

directions: Plant in unamended fast-draining soil on a warm slope. It withstands heat and needs no summer irrigation once rooted. Small whitish flowers in late winter give way to the large seed capsules that name it.

planting.note: A spiny gray-stemmed shrub for wild dry gardens.

directions: Plant where its stout thorny branches can form a thicket in dry soil. Provide full sun and no summer water once established. Fragrant white flower clusters cover the whitish stems in spring.

planting.note: A pale-stemmed spiny native for hot, dry slopes.

directions: Plant into lean dry ground with sharp drainage and full exposure. The rigid spiny branches form good wildlife cover. Pale blue to white flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: An adaptable oak-woodland shrub for dry gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun to very light shade. It takes to garden conditions more readily than many wild ceanothus. Deep blue to purple flower clusters appear in early spring.

planting.note: A tall San Diego native prized for vivid flower color.

directions: Plant in fast-draining soil in full sun and keep it dry in summer. Give it room, as it grows tall and open. Long-stalked clusters of bright blue flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: One of the most reliable garden hybrids; plant in autumn.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil, setting the crown at or slightly above grade. Little care is needed beyond light shaping just after bloom, never into old bare wood. Reddish buds open to a mass of deep-blue flowers in mid spring.

planting.note: A dark-flowered favorite for a sunny, dry border.

directions: Plant in sharply drained soil and keep summer water to a minimum. Prune only lightly after flowering to preserve the arching form. Tiny crinkled leaves set off intensely dark cobalt-blue flowers in spring.

planting.note: A dense mounding hybrid smothered in flower each spring.

directions: Plant in full sun with excellent drainage and refrain from summer irrigation once established. Shear only the tips lightly right after flowering. Small dark leaves nearly disappear under dense deep indigo-blue bloom in spring.

planting.note: A vigorous large hybrid often trained as a small tree.

directions: Give this big grower ample room and stake young plants toward a single trunk if a tree form is desired. It tolerates a bit more garden water than most ceanothus. Large medium-blue flower spikes open in early spring.

planting.note: A tall upright hybrid with frosted-looking flowers.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and keep the crown dry. Prune lightly after bloom to keep it shapely without cutting old wood. Glossy leaves carry medium-blue flowers frosted white in mid spring.

planting.note: A compact, later-blooming selection good for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant in fast-draining soil and tolerate a little more summer water than most ceanothus. Its tidy size suits containers and small beds. Deep blue flowers appear later than many, in late spring into early summer.

planting.note: One of the hardier evergreen selections; can be wall-trained.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and, in colder areas, shelter it against a warm wall. Prune lightly after flowering, avoiding cuts into leafless wood. Dark glossy leaves back rich blue flower clusters in late spring.

planting.note: A hardy, free-flowering selection for cooler climates.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun, ideally with wall shelter in marginal zones. Trim lightly after bloom to keep it dense. Small dark leaves are covered in deep-blue flowers over a long spring season.

planting.note: A low, spreading hybrid good at the front of a bank.

directions: Plant on a well-drained slope where its broad low habit can spread. Keep summer water minimal once established. Bright blue flower clusters cover the plant in mid to late spring.

planting.note: A classic low, wide bank cover; plant in autumn.

directions: Space plants generously and let the low branches spread to cover slopes. Tolerates a little garden water but resents soggy soil. Glossy green leaves carry medium-blue flowers in spring.

planting.note: A dense, tidy groundcover selection of Point Reyes ceanothus.

directions: Plant on a bank or in a dry border and let it knit into a weed-smothering mat. Its dense growth needs little pruning. Small toothed leaves bear violet-blue flowers in spring.

planting.note: A mounding, spreading hybrid for large banks.

directions: Plant on a slope and let the low arching stems spread and layer. It tolerates modest garden water where drainage is good. Medium-blue flower spikes appear over a long spring season.

planting.note: A rare white-flowered form of blueblossom.

directions: Give this large grower room and plant in fast-draining soil. Prune only lightly after flowering to shape it. Bright green foliage is covered with pure white flower clusters in spring.

planting.note: A white-flowered coastal selection with a broad habit.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and keep the crown above grade. Light shaping after bloom keeps it dense. Glossy leaves carry fragrant white flower spikes in spring.

planting.note: A variegated-leaved selection grown for its gold-edged foliage.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun, which brings out the brightest leaf coloring. Remove any all-green reverting shoots as they appear. Green-and-gold variegated leaves are topped with medium-blue flowers in spring.

planting.note: A low variegated groundcover grown mainly for foliage.

directions: Plant in light shade in hot climates to protect the bright leaves from scorch. It prefers a bit more moisture than typical ceanothus but still needs good drainage. Vivid gold-and-green foliage carries occasional pale-blue spring flowers.

planting.note: A hardy summer-blooming deciduous hybrid; prune in early spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and, because it flowers on new wood, cut stems back hard in early spring. It tolerates ordinary garden care better than the evergreen species. Soft powder-blue flower panicles appear from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: A pink-flowered deciduous hybrid blooming on new growth.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and prune stems back in early spring to encourage strong flowering wood. Deadhead spent clusters to prolong the display. Soft pink flower plumes appear from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: A compact hardy deciduous hybrid for smaller borders.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and cut back the previous year's stems in early spring since bloom comes on new wood. Its compact habit suits mixed borders and large pots. Deep blue flower clusters open through summer into early autumn.

planting.note: A hardy evergreen hybrid noted for a long late flowering season.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun, with wall shelter helpful in cold gardens. Trim lightly after the main flush to tidy without cutting old wood. Glossy leaves carry sky-blue flowers in summer with a strong repeat into autumn.

planting.note: A dependable long-blooming evergreen hybrid.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun and prune lightly after the first flush to keep it compact. It flowers over a notably long season. Small glossy leaves carry rich blue flowers from summer into autumn.

planting.note: A large tree-ceanothus selection with fragrant flowers.

directions: Give this vigorous grower plenty of room or train it against a warm wall. Shape young plants toward a single trunk for a small-tree effect. Large fragrant deep-blue flower panicles open in spring.

planting.note: A tall hybrid with gracefully arching flowering branches.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and give the arching branches space to cascade. Prune only lightly after bloom to keep the graceful habit. Powder-blue flower clusters hang along the branches in late spring.

planting.note: An early, cold- and clay-tolerant selection for tougher sites.

directions: Plant in full sun; this selection handles heavier soil and cold better than most ceanothus. Prune lightly after flowering to shape. Holly-like leaves carry lavender-blue flowers very early in spring.

planting.note: A dark-leaved selection grown for foliage contrast.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil to deepen the near-black leaf color. Prune lightly after bloom to maintain the compact shape. Dark bronze-purple foliage sets off pale blue flowers in late spring.

planting.note: Set plants out after frost into gritty, fast-draining soil in the hottest spot you have.

directions: Sow seed barely covered in a sharp, sandy mix and keep warm until sprouts appear. Plant young specimens on a sunny slope where the sticky, resin-scented foliage can bake, and avoid rich soil or heavy feeding. Large white flowers marked with a dark basal blotch open in early summer, each lasting a single day.

planting.note: Plant out once frost has passed into lean, well-drained ground.

directions: Start seed warm in a free-draining mix and grow on before planting in full sun. The soft, felted grey leaves prefer poor dry soil and resent winter wet, so improve drainage before planting. Soft lilac-pink flowers with a yellow eye cover the bush from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Move seedlings outdoors after the last frost into sunny, gritty soil.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep the pot warm and lightly moist until germination. This compact Mediterranean shrub thrives on neglect in hot, dry sites and needs sharp drainage to overwinter. Crinkled rose-pink blooms with a boss of golden stamens appear through early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into dry, sunny banks where it can spread.

directions: Sow warm in gritty compost and prick out seedlings once they can be handled. This low, spreading species makes a tough groundcover for baking slopes and tolerates coastal wind and poor soil. Small white flowers centered with yellow stamens open across the plant in late spring.

planting.note: Set out young plants after frost into hot, sharply drained soil.

directions: Germinate seed at warm temperatures in a fast-draining mix and grow on before planting. The narrow, sticky, dark green leaves suit dry Mediterranean gardens and the shrub shrugs off drought once rooted. Clusters of small white flowers wreathe the stems in early summer.

planting.note: One of the hardiest rockroses; plant in spring into well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow seedlings on in deep pots before planting out. This is among the most cold-tolerant species, forming a large upright shrub that withstands harder frosts than most rockroses. White flowers with a golden center open in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into gritty soil in the sunniest spot available.

directions: Start seed warm and move on seedlings once well rooted. This low, mounding species has wavy grey-green leaves and thrives on hot, poor, dry ground. Vivid magenta-pink flowers appear in flushes from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost into very free-draining, sunny soil.

directions: Sow seed warm in a sharp mix and keep barely moist. With its fine, rosemary-like leaves this species suits dry gravel gardens and demands excellent drainage. Small white flowers open in clusters through early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a warm, well-drained border.

directions: Germinate seed warm and grow on before planting into free-draining soil. The broad, poplar-like leaves are unusual among rockroses and this upright shrub tolerates a little more shade than most. White flowers with yellow-flushed bases open in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or containers after frost into hot, dry soil.

directions: Take semi-ripe cuttings in summer and root them in a gritty mix under cover. This popular hybrid needs full sun and sharp drainage and should be planted young since it dislikes root disturbance. Deep rose-pink flowers with a maroon blotch at each petal base open through summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into lean, fast-draining, sunny ground.

directions: Root semi-ripe cuttings in summer and pot on before planting the following year. Give this cultivar full sun and gritty soil, avoiding rich feeding that softens growth. White flowers each marked with a deep maroon basal blotch open in succession through summer.

planting.note: Set out after frost into hot, well-drained soil.

directions: Propagate from summer cuttings rooted in a sandy medium. This vigorous selection wants full sun and sharp drainage and needs no supplementary water once established. Rich reddish-pink flowers with dark central blotches cover the shrub in early summer.

planting.note: One of the tougher hybrids; plant in spring into free-draining soil.

directions: Take semi-ripe cuttings in late summer and overwinter the young plants under cover. This hardy old favorite forms a neat mound and tolerates coastal exposure and poor soil. Clear silvery-pink flowers with golden stamens open freely through early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into a hot, sharply drained position.

directions: Root semi-ripe cuttings in summer in a gritty mix. This low, spreading cultivar has grey-green wavy leaves and makes an excellent front-of-border or bank plant in dry gardens. Vibrant magenta-pink flowers with a yellow center smother the plant in early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost where the low mound can sprawl in full sun.

directions: Take summer cuttings and root them under cover for planting the next year. This wide, low-growing hybrid works well as groundcover on dry slopes and tolerates salt spray. White flowers with a crimson blotch at each petal base open all summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into gritty, sun-baked soil.

directions: Propagate from semi-ripe summer cuttings in a free-draining medium. This graceful hybrid has soft grey-green foliage and a bushy habit suited to dry, sunny borders. Masses of small, pale pink flowers open over a long stretch of early summer.

planting.note: One of the hardier hybrids; plant in spring into well-drained soil.

directions: Root semi-ripe cuttings in summer and grow on under shelter over winter. This dependable, relatively hardy hybrid forms a broad mound and tolerates cold and coastal sites better than most. Crimson-tinged buds open to pure white flowers with a yellow eye through early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a hot, free-draining border.

directions: Take semi-ripe cuttings in summer and root in gritty compost. This tall, vigorous hybrid has sticky aromatic foliage and stands up to frost and wind better than many rockroses. Large white flowers, each petal blotched crimson at the base, open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into the hottest, best-drained spot available.

directions: Root semi-ripe cuttings in summer and pot on before planting out. This large, striking hybrid has wavy green leaves and needs full sun with sharp drainage to flower well. Large white flowers each marked with crimson basal spots open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into sunny, sharply drained soil.

directions: Take semi-ripe cuttings in late summer and root them under cover. Give this compact cultivar full sun and lean, gritty soil for the best flowering. Large white flowers, each with a bold dark red blotch, open through early and mid summer.

planting.note: A hardy compact hybrid; plant in spring into free-draining soil.

directions: Root semi-ripe cuttings in summer and overwinter the young plants under shelter. This low, dense cultivar makes reliable groundcover and tolerates cold better than most rockroses. Small white flowers with a golden center smother the mound over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained, sunny ground.

directions: Propagate from semi-ripe summer cuttings rooted in a gritty mix. This low, spreading cultivar has soft grey-green leaves and copes with coastal wind and poor soil. Delicate pale pink flowers with a yellow eye open freely through early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into sunny, free-draining soil for best leaf color.

directions: Take semi-ripe cuttings in summer and root under cover. This variegated cultivar shows gold-edged foliage that colors best in full sun with lean soil. Yellow flowers with orange centers open above the bright leaves in early summer.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into hot, dry, sharply drained soil.

directions: Sow seed warm in gritty compost and grow on before planting in a sunny spot. This spreading, grey-felted shrub thrives on baking dry banks and dislikes winter wet. Golden-yellow flowers, often with a dark blotch at each petal base, cover the plant in late spring.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost into lean, gritty, sunny soil.

directions: Surface-sow seed and keep warm until seedlings appear, then grow on in deep pots. This upright, grey-leaved shrub wants full sun and free drainage and resents rich soil. Bright yellow flowers, each petal marked with a dark brown-red spot, open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into a hot, coastal, free-draining site.

directions: Germinate seed warm in a sandy mix and pot on the seedlings. This silvery-leaved shrub is well suited to seaside gardens and tolerates salt wind and drought. Yellow flowers, sometimes with dark basal spots, open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into gritty, sun-baked soil.

directions: Sow seed warm and grow seedlings on before planting in full sun. This small, fine-leaved shrub suits rock and gravel gardens and needs excellent drainage. Small white flowers open in clusters through late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into hot, free-draining soil in full sun.

directions: Take semi-ripe cuttings in summer and root in a gritty medium under cover. This low, compact cultivar makes a tidy front-of-border plant for dry sunny sites. Bright yellow flowers with a dark central blotch open over a long stretch of summer.

planting.note: A hardy low hybrid; plant in spring into free-draining soil.

directions: Root semi-ripe cuttings in summer and overwinter the young plants under shelter. This tough intergeneric hybrid forms a low carpet of narrow dark green leaves and tolerates cold and coastal exposure. Pure white flowers with a yellow eye cover the mat through summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into gritty, sunny, well-drained soil.

directions: Take semi-ripe cuttings in summer and root them under cover. This low, spreading hybrid has grey-green foliage and needs sharp drainage to overwinter well. Distinctive cream-white flowers marked with maroon and yellow zones open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into sharply drained, sunny soil.

directions: Root semi-ripe cuttings in late summer and grow on under shelter over winter. This selection has the same low, grey-leaved habit and needs excellent drainage to thrive. Soft cream-yellow flowers with maroon and gold central markings open in early summer.

planting.note: A hardy carpeting hybrid; plant in spring into free-draining ground.

directions: Take semi-ripe cuttings in summer and overwinter the rooted plants under cover. This low, spreading hybrid makes durable evergreen groundcover for dry banks and tolerates cold well. Small white flowers open across the mat through early and mid summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost into a hot, dry, well-drained border.

directions: Sow seed warm in a gritty mix and grow on before planting in full sun. This handsome shrub has broad silvery-grey leaves and needs sharp drainage and a sheltered warm spot. Golden-yellow flowers on branching stems open through late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot with morning sun and some afternoon shade.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the root ball and twice as wide, then backfill and water in well. Prune to a low framework in late winter since flowers and fruit form on the current season's growth. White summer blooms mature into clusters of pearly white berries that glow against the green foliage in fall.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants in spring; this cultivar is grown from cuttings to stay true.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil enriched with a little compost and water regularly the first summer. Cut stems back by at least a third in late winter to keep the plant dense and productive. It carries soft pink flowers followed by unusual pale pink berry clusters that persist into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade for the heaviest fruit set.

directions: Give this upright shrub room to arch and plant it where its fall color will be seen. Grouping several plants nearby improves cross-pollination and berry yield. Small lilac summer flowers ripen into dense clusters of shining violet berries that last well after the leaves drop.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring; propagated from cuttings for reliable heavy fruiting.

directions: Site in full sun with fertile, free-draining soil for the best show of berries. Prune the previous year's stems back in late winter to keep growth vigorous and flowering strong. This selection is prized for its exceptionally heavy crop of glossy purple berries that clothe the bare autumn stems.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring for a compact, early-fruiting shrub.

directions: Plant this tidy, mounded selection where its fall berries can be admired up close. Shear back hard in late winter to maintain the neat form and heavy fruit set. It colors early, dangling profuse clusters of amethyst-purple berries along arching branches before many other beautyberries ripen.

planting.note: Plant in spring; noted for fruiting reliably even as a young plant.

directions: This dwarf form suits small gardens and containers, so plant it in a spot with good drainage. Prune in late winter to encourage the fresh growth that carries the flowers. Even young plants set heavy crops of lilac-purple berries, making it a dependable choice for quick fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a location with morning sun for the whitest berries.

directions: Plant this white-fruited form where the pale berries can be seen against darker foliage or a wall. Cut back in early spring to renew the flowering wood. Modest white summer blooms give way to strings of small ivory berries that line the arching stems in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the variegated foliage gets some shelter from harsh afternoon sun.

directions: Grow this variegated selection in bright light with a little afternoon shade to keep the leaf edges crisp. Prune in late winter to shape it and stimulate flowering shoots. Green-and-cream foliage carries the plant through summer before pale white berries appear along the stems in fall.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock in spring in a spot with reliable summer moisture.

directions: Plant this white-berried form where the pale fruit stands out against a green backdrop. Prune in late winter to keep the plant open and vigorous. Small white summer flowers give way to clusters of translucent white berries that hold on the branches into fall.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun for the darkest foliage and heaviest fruit.

directions: Choose a sunny site so the dusky purple new foliage develops its richest color. Cut the shrub back by about a third in late winter to promote dense flowering growth. Pink summer flowers open against the dark leaves, followed by abundant clusters of amethyst berries in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; upright habit suits mixed borders and hedges.

directions: Plant this compact, upright selection in full sun to bring out the deep purple-black foliage. Trim lightly in late winter to keep its tidy vase shape and encourage bloom. Dark leaves set off pink summer flowers, which mature into tight clusters of violet-purple berries along the stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot with fertile, moist soil.

directions: Give this tall, narrow-leaved species room and a position out of drying winds. Prune older wood in late winter to keep it renewing from the base. Its long, slender leaves are joined in autumn by clusters of glossy violet berries that stud the upright stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered position; less cold-hardy than most beautyberries.

directions: Site this tender species where it is protected from hard frost and cold wind. Water through dry spells and prune lightly in late winter to shape it. Softly hairy foliage and pinkish flowers are followed by clusters of small purple-pink berries in autumn.

planting.note: Set out in spring or early fall in acidic, humus-rich soil.

directions: This hybrid of the dwarf and large species takes an intermediate size, so give it room to reach five to seven feet. Plant in moist, well-drained acidic soil and site in full sun to light shade for reliable bloom and fall display. Fragrant white brushes appear in spring, and the leaves color richly in orange and red before dropping.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a dependable, uniform selection.

directions: Plant this popular selection in acidic, well-drained soil with the root ball crown at grade and mulch generously. Prized for heavy spring bloom and consistent, vivid autumn color, it performs best in full sun with steady moisture. Watch for abundant honey-scented white bottlebrush flowers in spring and a blaze of orange, yellow, and red foliage in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; grown for its powder-blue summer foliage.

directions: A blue-leaved sport of 'Mount Airy', it wants acidic, moist, well-drained soil and holds its powder-blue summer color best with some protection from harsh afternoon sun. Plant at grade, mulch, and water through dry spells. Fragrant white spikes open in spring, and the blue foliage shifts to orange and red tones in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a compact form with blue-green leaves.

directions: This compact dwarf selection carries soft blue-green summer foliage and stays under three feet, fitting well in small beds and borders. Give it acidic, humus-rich soil that stays evenly moist, and site in part shade to preserve the blue cast of the leaves. Its fall color tends to run milder than green-leaved types, and honey-scented white flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a low, mounded, floriferous dwarf.

directions: A low, densely branched dwarf that blooms heavily even when young, 'Suzanne' works well at the front of a bed or in mass plantings. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil, mulch to keep roots cool, and give full sun for the strongest fall color. Expect fragrant white spring flowers and a reliable mix of orange, yellow, and red autumn foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a miniature, slow-growing form for small spaces.

directions: This miniature, slow-growing selection stays under two feet and suits rock gardens, troughs, and tight borders. Plant in moist, acidic, well-drained soil and avoid letting it dry out, since the compact root system is shallow. Small honey-scented white brushes appear in spring, and the tiny leaves color warmly in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; selected for outstanding autumn color.

directions: Bred for exceptional fall display, this upright selection lights up in intense orange and red as the days shorten. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil in full sun and keep it evenly watered through the first seasons. Fragrant white bottlebrush flowers open in spring ahead of the show that gives it its name.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a compact companion to Legend of the Fall.

directions: A more compact selection that keeps the strong fall color in a tidier three-to-four-foot frame. Plant in moist, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun for the best foliage display and mulch to steady soil moisture. Fragrant white spring flowers are followed by vivid orange and red autumn leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; a narrow-leaved dwarf with superb color.

directions: Noted for narrow, refined leaves and outstanding autumn color, this dwarf selection stays low and spreading. Plant in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil, mulch, and provide full sun to maximize the fiery fall foliage. Slender white bottlebrush flowers with a honey fragrance appear in spring before the leaves fully expand.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the rootball and twice as wide, then water in well and mulch to hold moisture. This selection carries longer flower spires than the species and turns an intense wine-red in autumn. Enjoy the drooping white blooms in early summer and let the fall color linger into winter in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in average to moist soil.

directions: Set this compact form at its original depth and water regularly through the first season to settle the roots. Its small mounded habit suits foundation beds and mass plantings where the full-size species would crowd. Fragrant white spires appear in late spring, and the foliage flushes bright red-orange in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant level with the surrounding soil and keep evenly watered while the shrub establishes. This dense, rounded selection holds its deep wine-red fall color reliably and stays more compact than older forms. Watch for fragrant white flower spikes in early summer that draw bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in moist soil.

directions: Position at the same depth as the nursery pot and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. This form is prized for unusually strong fragrance and improved bud hardiness, so it flowers dependably even after cold winters. Cut a few spires for the vase when the tiny white bells open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in average to moist soil.

directions: Plant at grade and water in thoroughly, then keep the soil from drying out during the first summer. This selection stands out for its stiff, upright spires that point skyward rather than arching down, giving a tidy vertical accent. Expect fragrant white flowers in early summer and rich red-purple leaves in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the shrub at its original growing depth and provide steady moisture as it roots in. This form is grown chiefly for its vivid autumn display, blending scarlet, orange, and gold across the same plant. Fragrant white summer spires precede the fireworks of fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in moist ground with room to spread.

directions: Plant at grade and allow this low, wide-spreading form to knit into a groundcovering colony by way of its suckers. It tolerates damp sites and part shade, making it useful along ponds and shady borders. Slender white flower racemes open in early summer above glossy foliage that reddens in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a sheltered, sunny wall in cooler zones.

directions: Site this broadleaf evergreen where it is protected from cold drying winds, ideally trained against a warm wall in marginal climates. Its dark, holly-like leaves stay glossy year-round, and it responds well to gentle pruning after bloom. Long, dangling catkin-like tassels of tiny greenish-white flowers drape the plant in mid to late summer with a soft honey scent.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot in mild-winter regions.

directions: Give this Chinese species a warm, protected position and well-drained but not droughty soil. Its slender arching branches carry narrow evergreen leaves that take on bronze tints in cold weather. Pendulous sprays of small creamy flowers appear in summer, lending a fine-textured presence to a woodland-edge border.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist ground; this dwarf selection stays low and mounded.

directions: Site this compact form in moist, acidic soil and keep it watered through dry spells. Prune only lightly in late winter since flowers form on new growth. Expect dense clusters of honey-scented white spikes in July and August, followed by good yellow fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this is the deepest-pink-flowered summersweet.

directions: Give it steadily moist, organic soil and part shade for the best flower color, which holds without fading. Water well the first two seasons to establish the roots. Fragrant rose-pink spikes appear in mid to late summer above glossy dark leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tidy dwarf with upright, non-nodding flower spikes.

directions: Plant in evenly moist soil and mulch to keep the shallow roots cool. Its stiff, erect white spikes stand up like candles rather than arching over. Enjoy the strong sweet fragrance in mid to late summer, then bright yellow leaves in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; pink buds open to soft-pink flowers.

directions: Set in moist, acidic soil where it can receive morning sun and afternoon shade. Keep the ground consistently damp, as this shrub tolerates wet sites well. Rosy-pink buds open to fragrant pale-pink spikes across mid and late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an older pink-flowered form with dark buds.

directions: Plant in rich, moisture-retentive soil and avoid letting it dry out. Deep-pink buds open to blush flowers that pale as they age. The fragrant spikes bloom in summer and are followed by clean yellow autumn foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; sold as Vanilla Spice with extra-large white flowers.

directions: Grow in consistently moist, well-drained soil and water regularly while establishing. This selection carries especially large, showy white spikes with a rich vanilla scent. Bloom peaks in mid to late summer and pairs with golden fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a very compact selection sold as Sugartina.

directions: Use this small mounded form at the front of a moist border or foundation planting. Keep the soil damp and mulched, and prune only to shape after bloom. Fragrant white spikes cover the plant in summer, and the leaves turn clear yellow in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; prized for blooming later than most summersweets.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil and keep it watered through late summer. This form extends the season with fragrant white spikes opening into early fall. Its later bloom makes it a valuable nectar source when few other shrubs are flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tall, large-flowered white form.

directions: Give this upright shrub room and steady moisture in an acidic, organic soil. It produces long white spikes with an intense sweet fragrance in late summer. Cut a few flowering stems for the vase without harming next year's bloom, which forms on new wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown for cream-splashed variegated leaves.

directions: Site in part shade to protect the variegated foliage from scorch, and keep the soil evenly moist. Remove any all-green shoots that revert to preserve the cream-and-green pattern. White fragrant spikes appear in summer above the marbled leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a dense, smaller-growing white form.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic ground and mulch to conserve water. This tidy selection keeps a rounded habit with little pruning. Fragrant white spikes open in mid to late summer, followed by yellow fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; sold as Einstein for its wild, spidery flower spikes.

directions: Grow in consistently moist soil in sun to part shade. The airy, twisted white flower spikes have an untidy, exploded look that gives the cultivar its name. Blooms are strongly fragrant in mid to late summer and attract many pollinators.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a clean white-flowered selection.

directions: Plant in rich, moisture-holding soil and water through dry summer weeks. It bears crisp white fragrant spikes in mid to late summer. Leave the dark seed capsules for winter interest or trim them off in spring before new growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a soft-pink flowered form.

directions: Set in moist, acidic soil in part shade for the truest pink color. Water consistently, especially while the plant establishes. Fragrant pale-pink spikes open through mid and late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a native mountain species with cinnamon bark.

directions: Grow this southern Appalachian native in moist, humus-rich woodland soil with dappled light. Its polished, peeling cinnamon-brown bark is a standout winter feature. Nodding, fragrant white flower spikes appear in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost danger passes; tender evergreen from Madeira.

directions: Grow in a frost-free garden or a cool greenhouse in acidic, well-drained soil. Protect it from hard freezes, as this evergreen tolerates only light frost. Fragrant white bell-shaped flowers resembling lily-of-the-valley open in nodding sprays in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot; a showy Chinese species.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, well-drained soil in a position protected from cold winds and late frosts. This species bears long, arching one-sided racemes of white cup-shaped flowers from pink buds. Bloom comes in mid to late summer, and young plants benefit from winter shelter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a late-blooming southern relative of summersweet.

directions: Plant in wet to moist, acidic soil where its suckering habit can form a thicket. The leaf undersides are covered in soft white wool that gives the plant its name. Fragrant white spikes open later than common summersweet, into late summer and early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered site; a Mexican evergreen species.

directions: Grow in mild-winter gardens in moist, well-drained, acidic soil with some protection from hard frost. This large evergreen shrub or small tree carries fragrant white flower spikes. Bloom occurs in summer and early fall on the current season's growth.

planting.note: Set out container plants in spring once hard frost has passed so roots establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil with the crown level with the surrounding ground and water regularly the first season to establish. Prune in late winter to shape the arching branches, since flowers form on new growth. Expect clusters of small tubular white-pink blooms from early summer through fall that draw bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost in a sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: Dig a hole twice the width of the root ball and backfill with loosened native soil, keeping the base slightly high. Cut back a third of the oldest stems in late winter to keep the plant open and vigorous. Lavender-pink funnel flowers open through the warm months against bronze-tinged new foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade as a low edger or foundation plant.

directions: Space plants so the compact mounds can knit into a low informal hedge, keeping the root ball even with the soil. Shear lightly after the main flush to keep the form dense. White-margined leaves flush pink in cool weather, and pale flowers appear through summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring; tolerates heat and humidity well once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained ground and mulch the base to conserve moisture through summer. Prune in late winter to renew the dense, rounded habit. Masses of small white flowers cover the plant from midsummer to frost, and bronze fall foliage follows.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a compact filler or low hedge.

directions: Site in full sun to part shade with the root flare at grade and water deeply during establishment. Shape lightly in early spring to maintain the fine-textured, dwarf mound. Small pinkish-white flowers appear through summer above glossy dark green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low groundcover on banks or the front of borders.

directions: Space plants generously so the spreading branches can cover ground and control erosion, keeping the crown at soil level. Prune only to remove stray upright shoots and keep the profile low. Delicate white flowers dot the mat through summer, with leaves turning purplish in autumn cold.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the richest golden leaf color.

directions: Give this variety full sun so the yellow-and-green variegation stays vivid, and plant with the crown even with the ground. Remove any all-green reverted shoots as they appear, and thin old wood in late winter. Fragrant white flowers open through the warm season against golden foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the best flower and foliage color.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist the first summer while roots spread. Prune in late winter to encourage fresh flowering wood. Apricot-yellow blooms open along the arching stems from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to keep the gold-margined foliage bright.

directions: Position in full sun so the bold yellow leaf margins hold their color, and set the plant at its original growing depth. Trim reverted green shoots and shape lightly in early spring. Fragrant white flowers appear in summer above the golden variegated leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a colorful low mound in sun.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed with the crown at grade and water through the establishment season. Shear lightly after the first bloom flush to keep the tri-colored mound dense. Green, cream, and pink variegated foliage sets off small white flowers through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to develop the copper new growth.

directions: Give this upright variety room to reach full size and plant with the root flare level with the soil. Renew a few old canes each late winter to keep growth fresh. New foliage emerges coppery-gold and matures green, with fragrant pinkish flowers from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where the fragrant late blooms can be enjoyed.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and allow space for a rounded, arching form. Cut back hard in late winter if needed, since bloom comes on new wood. Highly fragrant white-pink flower clusters cover the shrub in late summer and draw pollinators heavily.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this species is the hardiest abelia and takes colder zones.

directions: Site in sun to part shade with the crown at grade and give it room for a large arching shrub. Prune right after flowering because blooms form on the previous year's wood. Intensely fragrant pink buds open to white star flowers in late spring, followed by good red fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered spot as it is more frost-tender than glossy abelia.

directions: Give this shrub a protected, sunny location and well-drained soil, keeping the base at grade. In cold-edge zones treat it as a dieback plant and cut damaged stems in spring. Large lilac-pink bell flowers hang along arching stems through summer and into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for crisp white-and-green variegation.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed and keep the crown even with the soil surface. Shear lightly in early spring to keep the low mound compact and full. Bright white leaf margins with a green center make a tidy edger, accented by small white summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the lime and yellow leaf edges.

directions: Position in full sun so the lime-green and gold variegation stays vibrant, planting at the original soil depth. Trim any reverted green stems and shape lightly each spring. Light pink flowers appear in summer against the bright foliage, which flushes orange in cool weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to light shade as an upright accent.

directions: Set out in well-drained soil with room for a tall, arching habit and keep the crown at grade. Thin older canes in late winter to renew flowering wood. Creamy-margined leaves with pink new growth back masses of fragrant pale flowers through summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered site or against a wall in mild climates.

directions: Grow in a frost-protected spot with good drainage, ideally trained against a warm wall in cooler areas. Prune after flowering to control the long, arching stems. Showy pendulous rose-red tubular flowers hang along the branches in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where the ruby-red calyces show best.

directions: Plant in a sunny bed with the crown level with the ground and water to establish. Prune lightly in late winter to shape the compact form. White flowers give way to deep red sepals that hold color on the plant well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for strong yellow variegation and heavy bloom.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, keeping the base even with the surrounding grade. Shape lightly in early spring and remove any reverted green shoots. Yellow-edged foliage frames abundant pink-white flowers that are a magnet for bees through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to part shade for bright white variegation.

directions: Set the plant at grade in well-drained soil and give it consistent moisture while establishing. Trim lightly in spring to keep the mound compact and dense. Crisp white-and-green foliage flushes pink in cool weather and pairs with soft white summer flowers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the brightest gold-edged leaves.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot with the crown at grade and mulch to hold moisture. Prune in late winter to renew flowering wood and tidy the mounded form. Golden-margined foliage takes on red-orange tints in fall and surrounds fragrant white summer blooms.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to keep the foliage a bright solid gold.

directions: Give this compact variety full sun so the leaves hold their vivid yellow color, planting at the original depth. Shear lightly after flowering to keep the dwarf mound neat. Bright golden foliage all season is joined by small white flowers in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the largest, showiest pink flowers.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil with the crown at grade and water regularly to establish. Prune in late winter since flowers form on new growth. Unusually large bell-shaped pink flowers hang along arching stems from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low hedge or foundation shrub.

directions: Set plants close together for a dense low hedge, keeping the base level with the soil. Shear in early spring and again lightly after the first flush to maintain shape. Small white-pink flowers bloom from summer to frost above glossy dark foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun to part shade in a woodland-edge setting.

directions: Plant this Japanese species in well-drained soil with the crown at grade and give it room to arch. Prune after flowering, since bloom is on the prior season's wood. Small orange-throated pale pink flowers open in late spring, followed by decorative reddish sepals.

planting.note: Set out container plants in early spring or fall while temperatures are mild.

directions: Plant with the root crown level with the surrounding soil and water deeply until roots establish. Renew old canes by cutting a third of the tallest stems to the ground each late winter to keep the clump full. Airy white flower panicles open in early summer, followed by clusters of red berries that persist through winter.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf clone from containers in spring or fall for the best root establishment.

directions: Space these mounding dwarfs so their rounded forms nearly touch for a low, uniform mass. They rarely need pruning, but remove any stray tall shoots at the base to hold the compact shape. Foliage turns brilliant lime-green in summer and flames to fiery red through cold weather; this selection seldom flowers or fruits.

planting.note: Set out potted plants in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this compact selection at the same depth it grew in the pot and mulch to conserve moisture. Its dense, mounding habit needs little pruning beyond removing an occasional errant cane. New growth emerges blue-green and matures to red-tinged tones, deepening to rich crimson in winter cold.

planting.note: Plant from containers in spring or fall as a low, spreading groundcover.

directions: Because this cultivar spreads by short rhizomes, give each plant room to knit into a continuous low carpet. Shear lightly in late winter only if you want to refresh the foliage. Fine-textured leaves take on orange-red and bronze hues in autumn and cold, making it a favorite groundcover under taller shrubs.

planting.note: Set out in spring or fall in average, well-drained garden soil.

directions: Plant this fuller, bushier form where you want the classic look at a smaller scale than the species. Thin the oldest canes to the ground in late winter to keep growth dense from the base up. Lacy foliage flushes reddish in winter, and mature clumps still bear white summer flowers and red berries.

planting.note: Plant potted specimens in spring or fall; tolerates poor soils once established.

directions: Set these stiff, dome-shaped dwarfs at pot depth and water through the first season to settle roots. They need almost no pruning, keeping a tight cushion form on their own. Coarse, cupped leaves turn deep reddish-purple in winter, brightest in full sun; this clone rarely blooms.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a spot with good drainage.

directions: Plant this rounded dwarf at the same level it sat in the nursery pot and mulch lightly. Its dense habit stays neat with little more than removal of stray canes. New leaves emerge lime-green and the whole plant colors to reddish-orange in autumn and winter cold.

planting.note: Set out container plants in spring or fall in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this upright, medium-tall selection where its deep foliage color will show against lighter backgrounds. Thin a few of the oldest canes each late winter to keep the clump from getting leggy. Fresh spring growth emerges deep purple-red, matures to green, and returns to rich purple in cold weather.

planting.note: Plant potted dwarfs in spring or fall as edging or low mass planting.

directions: Space these very low, spreading dwarfs closely to form a tidy front-of-border ribbon. Their mounding habit needs no routine pruning, only cleanup of any tall shoots. Constantly renewing red new growth keeps color all season, deepening in fall and winter; this compact clone does not fruit.
